parentQuestion: The aqueous humor is continuously replenished and flows from the ciliary process to drain in the scleral venous sinus.

scaffolds.q: The anterior segment of the eye contains a clear fluid called aqueous humor.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! It fills the space between cornea and lens.

scaffolds.q: The cornea and lens are transparent and cannot have blood vessels.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Blood vessels would scatter light and blur vision.

scaffolds.q: Without blood supply, these tissues need another way to get nutrients.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Aqueous humor delivers nutrients and removes waste by diffusion.

scaffolds.q: The ciliary body/process PRODUCES aqueous humor.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Ciliary epithelium actively secretes this fluid into the posterior chamber.

scaffolds.q: Aqueous humor flows from posterior chamber → pupil → anterior chamber.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! It's made behind the iris, flows through the pupil to the front.

scaffolds.q: Aqueous humor drains through the trabecular meshwork into the scleral venous sinus.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Scleral venous sinus = canal of Schlemm. Drains to episcleral veins.

scaffolds.q: Blocked drainage of aqueous humor causes elevated eye pressure (glaucoma).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! High intraocular pressure damages the optic nerve → blindness.

scaffolds.q: Aqueous humor: Ciliary process → posterior chamber → pupil → anterior chamber → scleral venous sinus.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This continuous flow nourishes avascular tissues and maintains eye pressure.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - aqueous flows from ciliary process to scleral venous sinus.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Continuous production and drainage maintains intraocular pressure (~15-16 mmHg) and nourishes cornea and lens.
